question: What is tokenization in the context of large language models?

answers.answer: Splitting text into smaller units that a model can process.
answers.feedback: Correct. LLMs process token IDs rather than raw characters or words.

answers.answer: Encrypting text so that only the model can read it.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Tokenization is an encoding step, not an encryption method.

answers.answer: Removing punctuation before sending text to a model.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Punctuation can itself become tokens and can affect token counts.
Not quite. Tokenization happens before text is processed by the model.

question: Why can two model families produce different token counts for the same text?

answers.answer: They may use different tokenizers and vocabularies.
answers.feedback: Correct. Token boundaries depend on the tokenizer used by the model.

answers.answer: Token counts are chosen randomly each time text is encoded.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Tokenization is deterministic for a given tokenizer and text.

answers.answer: All tokenizers count one token per word, but models display the counts differently.
answers.feedback: Not quite. A token can be a word, part of a word, punctuation, or another unit.
Not quite. Open and closed models both use tokenizers.

question: Why do token counts matter when using hosted LLM APIs?

answers.answer: They affect context-window limits and can affect usage cost.
answers.feedback: Correct. Inputs and outputs must fit into the model context window, and many hosted services bill by usage.

answers.answer: They determine whether the output is grammatically correct.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Token count is not a guarantee of output quality.

answers.answer: They determine the font size used in the notebook.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Tokens are internal text units, not display settings.

answers.answer: They only matter for non-English text.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Token counts matter for all text, although the ratio can vary by language and script.

question: What is a good reason to shorten a verbose prompt while preserving its meaning?

answers.answer: It can reduce token usage while keeping the model task clear.
answers.feedback: Correct. Clearer and shorter prompts can reduce cost and leave more room in the context window.

answers.answer: It forces the model to use a different licence.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Prompt length does not affect the model or package licence.

answers.answer: It guarantees that the model will produce a shorter answer.
answers.feedback: Not quite. Output length is usually controlled by separate generation settings.
Not quite. Any text sent to an LLM still needs to be tokenized.
